topic: mRNA Vaccine Efficacy vs. Social Media Claims

findings.claim: BNT162b2 demonstrates 95.3% efficacy in preventing symptomatic COVID-19 infection in Phase III trials with 43,548 participants.
findings.consensus: Peer-reviewed gold standard. Replicated across multiple independent studies.

findings.claim: Real-world effectiveness drops to 84-88% against Delta variant, consistent with expected immune escape from antigenic drift.
findings.consensus: Supports efficacy with expected variant-specific attenuation.

findings.claim: Vaccines are only 12% effective and cause more hospitalizations than they prevent.
findings.contradiction: Directly contradicts NEJM Phase III data (95.3%) and CDC surveillance (84-88%). The 12% figure appears to originate from a misread of an absolute risk reduction metric, not relative efficacy.

findings.claim: Adverse event rates are significantly underreported in VAERS due to passive surveillance bias.
findings.contradiction: Valid methodological concern about passive reporting, but the claim conflates reporting bias with actual harm rates. Does not invalidate efficacy data.

forensicConclusion: CRITICAL MISINFORMATION DETECTED. The viral claim (12% efficacy) is a statistical misrepresentation of absolute vs. relative risk reduction. Peer-reviewed consensus strongly supports 84-95% efficacy depending on variant. The preprint raises a valid but separate concern about adverse event surveillance methodology.
